note_data.title: Budgeting and budgetary control
keyPoints: Budgeting & budgetary control is a key topic in Cost & Management Accounting
Manufacturing accounts calculate the production cost of manufactured goods at the end of a financial period
Cost of production equals prime cost plus factory overheads
Transfer price is the production cost of completed goods plus a percentage mark-up
Factory/manufacturing profit is the difference between transfer price and production cost of completed goods
Unrealised profit is profit which is not recognised until certain conditions are met

definitions.definition: An account prepared at the end of a financial period to calculate the production cost of manufactured goods
The total of direct materials, direct labour, and direct expenses
Costs incurred from the running of the factory, including indirect factory wages and depreciation of factory machinery
The production cost of completed goods plus a percentage mark-up
The difference between the transfer price and the production cost of completed goods
Profit which is not recognised until certain conditions are met

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Show all workings clearly. Label favourable (F) or adverse (A) for variances
Calculate variances AND explain their significance to management

modelAnswer.question: Explain TWO advantages of using flexible budgets rather than fixed budgets for budgetary control purposes.
modelAnswer.answer: Flexible budgets provide more meaningful comparisons because they adjust budgeted costs to the actual level of activity, making variances more relevant for control purposes. This allows management to distinguish between variances caused by activity level changes and those caused by efficiency or price differences. Flexible budgets also provide better performance evaluation because managers are only held responsible for costs at their actual level of operations, rather than being penalised for activity levels outside their control.
markBreakdown: Mark 2: Because costs adjusted to actual activity level
Mark 3: Better performance evaluation of managers
Mark 4: Because removes impact of activity level changes

commonMistakes: Including non-production costs like administration, finance, and distribution costs in the manufacturing account instead of the income statement
Confusing prime cost with total production cost
Not understanding the difference between transfer price and production cost
examinerTips: Remember that manufacturing accounts only include factory and actual manufacturing process information
Ensure you can calculate cost of production using the formula: prime cost + factory overheads
formulas: Sales Price Variance = (Actual price - Budgeted price) × Actual quantity sold
Sales Volume Variance = (Actual quantity - Budgeted quantity) × Budgeted price
Material Price Variance = (Actual price - Standard price) × Actual quantity purchased
Material Usage Variance = (Actual quantity - Standard quantity) × Standard price
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Budget preparation flowchart showing: Sales budget → Production budget → Material/Labour/Overhead budgets → Master budget (Income statement and Balance sheet). Include feedback arrows showing variance analysis leading to corrective action.
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (8 marks) — they asked students to prepare a cash budget for 3 months and explain two benefits of budgetary control
Featured in O/N 2022 P2 Q3 (6 marks) — candidates had to calculate budget variances and suggest reasons for adverse variances
Appeared in M/J 2022 P1 Q15 (2 marks) — multiple choice question on the purpose of flexible budgets
Tested in O/N 2021 P2 Q5 (10 marks) — comprehensive question requiring preparation of production budget and discussion of zero-based budgeting

workedExample.question: ABC Ltd is preparing its cash budget for January to March 2024. Sales: Jan $50,000, Feb $60,000, Mar $70,000. 40% cash sales, 60% credit (collected next month). Purchases: Jan $30,000, Feb $35,000, Mar $40,000 (paid next month). Wages $8,000 monthly (paid same month). Opening cash balance $15,000. Prepare the cash budget. (8 marks)
steps: Step 1: Calculate cash receipts - Jan: $50,000 × 40% = $20,000 cash sales only (no opening debtors given)
Step 4: Calculate cash payments - Jan: wages $8,000 only (no opening creditors given)
Step 7: Calculate monthly closing balances using: Opening + Receipts - Payments
workedExample.answer: Cash Budget: Jan - Receipts $20,000, Payments $8,000, Closing balance $27,000; Feb - Receipts $54,000, Payments $38,000, Closing balance $43,000; Mar - Receipts $64,000, Payments $43,000, Closing balance $64,000
workedExample.markScheme: 2 marks for correct cash receipts calculation, 2 marks for correct payments, 2 marks for proper budget format, 2 marks for accurate closing balances

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused cash budgets with profit budgets by including non-cash items like depreciation' — always exclude depreciation and other non-cash expenses from cash budgets

Examiner Report 2022: 'Students frequently forgot to account for timing differences in credit sales and purchases' — always consider when cash is actually received/paid, not when sales/purchases occur

Examiner Report 2021: 'Weak responses failed to distinguish between adverse and favorable variances' — remember adverse variances are bad (actual costs higher than budget, actual revenue lower than budget)
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must draw a Budget Preparation Flow Chart showing: 1) Sales Budget at top (starting point), 2) Production Budget (units needed), 3) Direct Materials Budget, 4) Direct Labour Budget, 5) Overhead Budget, 6) Cash Budget (combining all cash flows), 7) Budgeted Income Statement, 8) Master Budget at bottom. Include arrows showing dependencies - sales budget feeds into production budget, which feeds into resource budgets, all feeding into cash budget.
note_data.quickRecap: Budgeting & budgetary control is part of Cost & Management Accounting. Manufacturing accounts calculate production costs by adding factory overheads to prime costs. Transfer pricing involves adding a mark-up to production costs, creating manufacturing profit which may be unrealised until goods are sold.
